Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs
Off Calendar Feb-10-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. MARK HOOSHMAND, ESQ. of Hooshmand Law Group, Tel No. 415-318-5709 appearing in pro per;
Atty. KAMBIZ ADIBZADEH, ESQ. of Adibzadeh Law Firm, Tel No. 415-225-0025 appearing for Defendant Karleen Griffin.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts the substance of its tentative ruling with modifications to certain language: Defendant Karleen Griffin's motion for fees and costs is granted in very small part. Ms. Griffen is awarded fees and costs of $2090, calculated as $2000 in fees for 10 hours at $200 per hour and $90 in costs consisting of the filing fee of $60 and court reporter fee of $30. Defendant's request for almost $100,000 in fees and costs is an "inflated" request which, under the anti-SLAPP statute as well as other statutes, is "a special circumstance permitting the trial court to reduce the award or deny one altogether." (Christian Research Institute v. Alnor (2008) 165 Cal. App. 4th 1315, 1321-22, quoting Ketchum v. Moses (2001) 24 Cal. 4th 1122, 1137. Among the many reasons why the fee request is vastly out of proportion to what it should have been are: 1) Ms. Griffin prevailed on only claim on her anti-SLAPP motion, 2) she did so on a basis that she did not address in her moving papers, 3) the issues on the claim for which she did prevail were, for anti-SLAPP purposes, both simple and fully resolved in her favor in a single case (Wong v. Tai Jing (2010) 189 Cal. App. 4th 1354), 4) the papers submitted by Ms. Griffin on both the anti-SLAPP motion and this motion for fees were needlessly long and convoluted, 5) the hugely excessive hours assertedly spent by Ms. Griffen's counsel, and 6) the very high hourly rates claimed by those attorneys compared to other sole practitioners and small firm lawyers who do similar work. As suggested by her appeal of the order on the motion, the transcript of the hearing and the order issued by Judge Quinn, in practical reality Ms. Griffen lost the motion on the only two claims of any real consequence, the libel and false light claims. Because plaintiff is able to recover emotional distress damages on the libel and false light claims and can do so without having to bear the heavy burden of proving that he suffered "severe" emotional distress (a requirement of an IIED claim), and under the facts of this case it is extremely unlikely that plaintiff could prove "outrageous conduct" by Ms. Griffen (the first element of an IIED claim) without also proving the elements of the libel and/or false light claims, the IIED claim added virtually nothing to Ms. Griffen's exposure. Recent case law establishes that where, as here, a defendant succeeds on some but not all of her anti-SLAPP motion, the "fees awarded to the defendant" should be commensurate with the extent to which the motion changed the nature and character of the lawsuit in a practical way." (Malin v. Singer (2013) 217 Cal. App. 4th 1283. 1305). Per the principles enunciated in Christian Research and Malin, it would be reasonable to deny any award of fees and costs, but instead the Court believes that the award of fees for ten hours at a rate commensurate with the skill level shown by the papers submitted by Ms. Griffen;s counsel and for the basic costs incurred for the hearing on the anti-SLAPP motion is more faithful to the mandatory wording of Code of Civil Procedure 425.16(c). ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)
